Precision measures the proportion of correctly identified positive instances out of the total predicted positives, while recall measures the proportion of actual positive instances that were correctly identified.

In other words, when trying to improve precision by reducing false negatives (missed detections), it generally results in more false positives (incorrectly labeled as positive). Conversely, improving recall by reducing false positives can lead to fewer true positives being missed.

This trade-off occurs because different metrics are important for different applications.
